issues.description: `lower_expr_iterative` is never called anywhere in the codebase. Ripgrep across the entire repo returns no call sites — only the definition in the diff. Introducing a non-trivial, bug-ridden helper that nothing invokes is pure dead weight: it cannot deliver the claimed stack-overflow protection, it increases maintenance surface, and it will silently rot.
issues.suggested_fix: Either wire it into `lower_expr` / the entry path so it actually runs, or drop the function entirely. If stack-overflow protection on deep expressions is the real goal, follow the existing pattern used by `find_named_member_span_depth` in crates/reify-syntax/src/lib.rs:221 (bounded recursion with a `MAX_*_DEPTH` constant like `MAX_MEMBER_NESTING_DEPTH = 32`).

issues.description: The function is not actually iterative. The `binary_expression` arm calls `self.lower_binary_expr(node)`, which in turn calls `self.lower_expr(left_node)?` and `self.lower_expr(right_node)?` at lines 1729-1730 — fully recursive. The `unary_expression` arm calls `self.lower_unary_expr`, which recurses via `lower_expr` at 1748. The `function_call` arm calls `lower_function_call`, which recurses via `lower_expr` at 2002. The `_` fallback calls `self.lower_expr(node)` directly. So every interior-node branch ultimately recurses through `lower_expr`, defeating the stated purpose (avoiding stack overflow) while still paying the cost of stack pushes.
issues.suggested_fix: A genuine iterative lowering requires a two-phase worklist: phase 1 flattens the AST into a post-order list of (node, kind) frames, phase 2 walks that list from leaves up, popping already-lowered children off a result stack to construct each parent. Alternatively, as noted above, use bounded recursion with a depth guard rather than reinventing this.

issues.description: `results.into_iter().last().flatten()` does not return the root. Trace a simple `a + b`: (1) push root_bin; (2) pop root_bin, push children [a, b], push recursive lower_binary_expr result into `results` → results=[BinOp{a,b}]; (3) pop b, push Ident(b) → results=[BinOp{a,b}, Ident(b)]; (4) pop a, push Ident(a) → results=[BinOp{a,b}, Ident(b), Ident(a)]. `.last()` returns `Ident(a)` — a leaf — instead of the root `BinOp{a,b}`. If this function is ever wired up, every non-trivial expression will silently lower to the wrong AST. This is a correctness bug, not a style issue.
issues.suggested_fix: Return `results.into_iter().next().flatten()` only if results are guaranteed to be ordered root-first (currently they are, because the root is processed first), OR redesign as a proper post-order worklist where the final stack top IS the root. The current combination of pre-order stack traversal + recursive-helper-for-interior-nodes + `.last()` is internally inconsistent.

issues.description: For `binary_expression`, children are pushed onto the stack AND `lower_binary_expr(node)` is called, which re-lowers both children recursively. Then the pushed children are popped and lowered a second time. Net effect: every binary node causes its subtree to be lowered twice (quadratic blow-up for nested operators). Same pattern applies implicitly via `lower_function_call` for argument nodes.
issues.suggested_fix: Either don't push children when calling the recursive helper, or don't call the recursive helper when pushing children — but do only one. The correct iterative design does the latter.

issues.description: No tests accompany this change. There is no regression test that constructs a pathologically deep expression (e.g. thousands of nested binary ops) and asserts that lowering (a) does not stack-overflow and (b) produces the correct AST. Without such a test, the 'avoids stack overflow on deeply nested expressions' claim is unverified and there's nothing to catch the wrong-return-value bug above.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a test in crates/reify-syntax/tests/ that builds a deep expression (e.g. `1+1+1+...` at 10k levels) and asserts successful lowering with a structurally correct result. Mirror the style of `member_span_tests.rs` which exercises MAX_MEMBER_NESTING_DEPTH boundary conditions.

issues.description: Compared to the authoritative `lower_expr` match at line 1687, `lower_expr_iterative` is missing handlers for: range_expression, conditional_expression, match_expression, lambda_expression, quantifier_expression, quantity_literal, list_literal, set_literal, map_literal, ad_hoc_selector, index_access, member_access, qualified_access, instance_qualified_access. All of these fall through to the `_ => self.lower_expr(node)` recursive branch, further undermining the iterative premise.
issues.suggested_fix: If the function is kept, enumerate all node kinds that `lower_expr` handles. Better: delete it and centralize on a single depth-bounded `lower_expr`.

issues.description: Even the leaf cases (number_literal, string_literal, bool_literal, identifier) duplicate dispatch that already lives in `lower_expr` at lines 1696-1699. Any future node kind added to the grammar now has two places to remember to update, and they will drift.
issues.suggested_fix: Delete the duplicated dispatch and reuse `lower_expr` (or extract a shared dispatch table if there's a real reason both must exist).

issues.description: The doc comment claims 'processes the AST using an explicit stack instead of recursion, avoiding stack overflow on deeply nested expressions.' The implementation does neither: it still recurses via helper functions and does not prevent stack overflow. A reader trusting the comment will be misled about the function's guarantees.
issues.suggested_fix: Rewrite the comment to match reality, or (preferably) rewrite the implementation to match the comment.

issues.description: The codebase already established a convention for handling pathological nesting: a `MAX_*_DEPTH` constant paired with a depth-counting recursive walk (see `MAX_MEMBER_NESTING_DEPTH = 32` and `find_named_member_span_depth` in crates/reify-syntax/src/lib.rs:205-226). This diff introduces a completely different approach (stack-based) without discussion, without matching the existing idiom, and without the guard-rail tests that the existing approach has (see `member_span_tests.rs:212-228`).
issues.suggested_fix: Follow the existing pattern: add `MAX_EXPR_NESTING_DEPTH` and thread a depth counter through `lower_expr`. Far less code, consistent with the crate's style, testable with the same boundary pattern already used elsewhere.

comprehensive_reviewer.summary: The new `lower_expr_iterative` function has multiple blocking defects and should not merge in its current form. It is dead code (no call sites), it is not actually iterative (all interior-node branches recurse through `lower_binary_expr`/`lower_unary_expr`/`lower_function_call`/`lower_expr`), it returns the wrong result (`results.last()` yields a leaf child, not the root — e.g. `a+b` would lower to `Ident(a)`), and it double-processes children of binary expressions. No tests accompany it, and the approach diverges from the crate's established pattern for pathological-input protection (`MAX_MEMBER_NESTING_DEPTH` + depth-counted recursion). The cleanest fix is to delete this function and, if deep-expression protection is genuinely needed, add a `MAX_EXPR_NESTING_DEPTH` guard to the existing `lower_expr` in line with `find_named_member_span_depth`.
